text: The surgeon uses a prograsp instrument in the second arm from the patient's right side to lift and reposition, while a fenestrated bipolar instrument is visible through the far lateral port.
The surgeon uses scissors inserted through the subxiphoid working port, creating an angle similar to that of a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to achieve a clear critical view of safety during the cholecystectomy, without the need for ICG green dye.
The surgeon identifies and cuts a bridging vein beneath the gallbladder, creating a wide triangular space in the closed triangle area.

The surgeon identifies the artery, initially positioned against the liver bed. The top arm is relaxed and retracted to pull the gallbladder away from the liver bed, improving visibility of the artery.
The surgeon attempts to visualize the one-third portion of the cystic plate, which is currently obscured in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a wristed instrument to grasp the underside of the gallbladder, pulling it away from the liver bed to expose more of the cystic plate. This manipulation provides a clear critical view of safety, enhancing visibility compared to traditional laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon manipulates the instruments to reposition the falciform ligament, moving it towards the belly button to facilitate easier access and movement during the procedure, using a technique similar to laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon applies a medium hemoclip to the end artery and then uses scissors to cut the cystic duct, completing the dissection of these structures.
The surgeon uses scissors to cauterize along the length of the artery, demonstrating the technique to the resident assisting in the procedure.
The surgeon manipulates the wristed instrument to address the intra-hepatic gallbladder, carefully adjusting the angle to avoid burning into the liver bed.
